raw.regions_all: Suriye; Irak; Mısır; Kuzey Afrika; İspanya; Horasan; Mâverâünnehir; Sind; Ermenistan; Kafkasya
raw.narrative_en: The Umayyad Caliphate transformed the Islamic state from a tribal republic into a centralized empire spanning the Mediterranean to Central Asia. The Damascus-based dynasty, founded by Mu'awiya, consolidated state identity under Abd al-Malik by making Arabic the official language and minting Islamic coinage. While conquests reached Spain and Sind, the mawali grievances and Ahl al-Bayt opposition deepened the regime's legitimacy crisis.

raw.notes: Imam of Nizari Ismailis; Prince Karim Aga Khan IV (d.2025), Prince Rahim Aga Khan V

raw.narrative_en: The 'Imād Shāhīs ruled briefly in Berar as the smallest of the Deccan sultanates. They were annexed by the Nizām Shāhīs.

The 'Ādil Shāhīs were a dynasty that established one of the Deccan's most powerful sultanates centered on Bijapur, building the Gol Gumbaz. The reign of Ibrāhīm 'Ādil Shāh II represents the pinnacle of cultural and artistic patronage. They ruled for approximately 200 years until Mughal Awrangzīb's conquests.
The Barīd Shāhīs ruled centered on Bidar as one of the Bahmanid successor sultanates. They ended with annexation by the 'Ādil Shāhīs.

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Nawwāb-Viziers and Kings of Oudh (Awadh)
raw.narrative_en: The Nawwābs of Awadh were a dynasty that created a wealthy Islamic cultural center with Shī'ī character centered on Lucknow. Lucknow's literary, musical, and architectural tradition took shape in this period. The British annexation in 1856 ignited one of the most violent fronts of the 1857 Great Rebellion a year later.

The Nizām Shāhīs were a Bahmanid successor sultanate centered on Ahmednagar, entering history through Chand Bībī's heroic resistance against the Mughals. Ruling for approximately 145 years, they were conquered by Mughal Shāhjahān.

The Nizāms of Hyderabad were a dynasty that established India's largest and wealthiest princely state by gaining independence from Mughal Deccan governorship. Maintaining independence until 1948, Hyderabad survived as a British ally and its incorporation into India was only achieved through 'Police Action.'

The Qutb Shāhīs were a dynasty of Turkmen origin that established one of the wealthiest Deccan sultanates centered on Golconda. Golconda's diamond mines — including the Koh-i Noor — produced world-famous treasures. Founding the city of Hyderabad and building the Charminar, the Qutb Shāhīs were the architects of the Telugu-Islamic cultural synthesis.
The Sharqī Sultans of Jawnpur were a small dynasty that separated from the Delhi Sultanate, ruling briefly and notable for their architectural legacy.

The Sultans of Gujarat established a powerful Islamic state centered on Ahmedabad on India's western coast, shaped by maritime trade and Portuguese rivalry. Ahmedabad's architectural legacy forms the most lasting trace of this era.

raw.parent_tribe: Şâh Mîr Svâtî ailesi; Çek ailesi
raw.narrative_en: The Sultans of Kashmir were a dynasty forming a distinctive branch of Islamic civilization in the sheltered Himalayan valley. Sultan Zayn al-'Ābidīn created Kashmir's golden age through his tolerance policy between Hindus and Muslims. The Kashmir shawl and handicraft traditions took root in this period.

raw.notes: Turkmen confederation

raw.regions_all: Balear Adaları; Doğu Mağrib (İfrîkıye bölgesinde faaliyetler)
raw.narrative_en: The Banu Ghaniya were the last remnant of the Almoravid dynasty in the Balearic Islands, maintaining independence as a resilient maritime principality for eighty years in the Mediterranean. They waged prolonged guerrilla warfare against the Almohads in eastern Maghreb, defending the declining Almoravid cause.

raw.parent_tribe: 1. Argun boyu (Moğol/Türk); 2. Muhammed Îsâ Tarhân kolu
raw.narrative_en: The Arghūns and Tarkhāns were a Turco-Mongol dynasty ruling in two branches centered on Thatta in Sind. They ended with Mughal Akbar's annexation of Sind.
